sentences.text: Not one word of English.
It was very, very difficult.
We had lessons all together, in groups.
But in the beginning, I thought I'll never learn English.
It was really difficult.
And then I stayed in, in Windermere.
Of course, they sent us to the sanatorium.
And there we were only two girls.
The rest were all boys.
We were five miles away from, from any little town, near-- five miles from Ashford, Kent.
And there in the sanatorium, we had to rest.
We really had to rest.
We were-- in the morning, we were woken up with breakfast.
And we had to stay in bed.
And just in the beginning, we stayed in bed.
And then, after a few weeks, we started getting up one hour between 11:00 and 12:00 and for one hour between 3:00
00 until we were getting-- because tuberculosis takes a long time to heal.
And they made us drink so much milk in those days and so much we had to consume and so on.
And, of course, I got very fat.
And a girl who came over from concentration camp who was so thin.
And I wanted-- they were going to treat me now to some clothes.
And I wanted a nice suit, a costume, you know?
And before they could get me the costume, they had to buy me corset first, because I got so fat in, in-- from the sanatorium.
And we were sleeping and eating.
And then, of course, we stayed there for nearly a year.
And we, we-- they sent us to a-- to a hostel in Kings Langley, which was run by girls of-- from some-- from some committees.
And this was a beautiful house taking over for us.
And we were looked after very well there.
We really lived well.
That means we had very good food, and we had-- we did nothing.
And all the time, we just could do what we wanted.
They took us out for outings in a coach.
They would take us to the seaside or show us this part or that part.
These girls.
I don't really know who they are, the committee.
I don't know, but I know that that was committee who was looking after this hostel.
Jewish girls.
And--
Oh, we got very, very, very friendly.
We were not like friends.
More like sisters, all of us.
Because we went through the same thing.
And really and truly, we couldn't talk about it.
We never talked about our experiences.

And, therefore, now when the book is here, this is what I'm finding out really about what's, what's happened to these friends or these-- because it was very painful to talk about our-- we could say maybe that I come from a big family, that I had maybe-- or where I come from and so on.
But we wouldn't go into big details because you couldn't talk about it really.
It was very painful.
And--
No, we were mainly trying to do other things.
There were table tennis, they had.
So we were playing table tennis or other games, or we used to amuse ourselves with the piano.
Some of the girls played the piano.
And we sang and we danced.
You know, we tried to enjoy ourselves, because it was a real like a real convalescent place, you know, that place.
And slowly, slowly-- that wasn't going to last forever.
I mean, they couldn't keep up such a place for very long.
So most of the girls were sent to Haddon Hill, somewhere around there to live there in a hostel.
But, to me, something has happened.
When I was in, in the sanatorium, suddenly, somebody came to see me.
And he was from my hometown because he knew my parents.
And when they called out Minia Munter in the sanatorium, I thought it might be somebody from my family.
It was a great shock.
I was very happy that somebody knows me in this place.
But it was also-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was happy, and I had a shock.
So when I came looking, yes, there was a man who knew-- he said to me straightaway, oh, yes, you're a Munter.
You look just like your father.
And he came with his wife and another couple from my hometown.
From that on-- from then onwards, he used to come often to see me with his wife to the sanatorium.
But then, when we came to Kings Langely-- that was very near London-- they used to come every second week to see me.
They used to invite me home.
And his wife was a corsetiere.
She used to make corsetry.
So he talked me into learning corsetry.
I mean, really and truly, when I came over here, I didn't know English, so it was difficult.
But on the other hand, I could've probably learned other things.
But he said to me, what do you want to learn?
They wanted to adopt, in fact.
And he wanted very much that I should learn corsetry.
So I said, well, I don't know English.
The people are so nice to me.
And it would be a good thing to know something.
You know, I learned corsetry.
And it helped me a lot to know it sometime in my life.
It really helped me.
And at the time when I learned corsetry-- because I learned corsetry, I had to come to live in London.
And this is how I came to live in a hostel in [INAUDIBLE].
And the girls in [INAUDIBLE] who lived there were from before the war they came.
I was the only girl who actually came from after the war.
And there was one girl who got-- particularly got very friendly with me.
She was from Germany.
And she talked me in one day.
She wanted very much I should go with her to Hyde Park.
Hyde Park?
I've never heard of Hyde Park.
I didn't even know it at all.
And she told me, there's going to-- there are going to be speakers from Israel, and we should go.
She drove me so much about it.
She told me in the morning, Minia, let's wash our hair.
Let's go.
Let's go.
Come on, let's dress up.
Let's go.
But in those days, I was already learning corsetry, and I was being paid by my employer, you know?
And he always tried to give me a little bit of extra, so that I shouldn't be so terribly short.
So and clothes was very cheap, so I managed to get myself a cloth-- something to wear here and there.
So, sure enough, I had something to wear.
In fact, we changed around.
She had something which she liked-- she, she-- very nice outfit.
But she wore it so often, she was already fed up.
She said, Minia, can I change with you?
I was wearing hers, and she was wearing mine.
What was her name?
The particular girl who wanted to change dress with me was Ruth.
But the girl who went with me was, was from Germany.
Ruth was from Holland.
But this girl was from Germany.
Her name was Ellen.
Helen?
Ellen?
Helen, I think.
Anyway.
So, anyway, we went.
And this girl from Holland, the girl who was wearing that outfit-- it was a Scottish outfit.
Red, green, these beautiful colors.
A waistcoat with a skirt.
It really fitted me.
She had the same figure as me.
By this time, I was slim.
I was not anymore fat.
I had really gone down completely because this was only in the sanatorium when I got-- afterwards, I went back to a slim figure.
Anyway.
So we went to this sanatorium-- we went to this Hyde Park.
We arrive in Hyde Park.
After there, I remember number 16 bus from Cricklewood.
A lot of people standing there, everybody surrounding these Israelis who were talking.
I spoke very little English.
I spoke a little bit already but very little.
And, suddenly, the, the, the man who talked about it was saying about the six million Jews being killed.
And I'm standing there.
And so many people.
At this moment, I'm standing here, and a German was standing-- a prisoner of war.
And he was standing.
It was an English man, a gentlemen with a bowler hat and umbrella, in those days, you know, with his car-- red carnation standing there.
And a few people were standing.
And he was saying [GERMAN], this is not true.
They're making more out than it is.
And I'm standing there.
I was holding myself like with both my-- you know, with all my strength not to say something.
Because if I say something, I knew I would have all the people around me in Hyde Park.
I could see what was happening, so I didn't want to say anything.
But at a certain point, when he was saying [GERMAN], this and that, I turned around to him.
And I said, [GERMAN], I said to him.
First of all, German I know.
I know how to say things.
You are-- you were eating bread.
My English was terrible, you know.
You were staying and eating English bread here while I was starving.
And my parents are dead.
And you say this is not true.
He was only a little fellow, this German.
And I was quite tall.
I had high heels as well.
In this moment, everybody was already standing around me.
And this German got started shaking and ran away.
I wanted to run after him.
And just as I'm standing there, everybody-- fraulein, fra-- miss, you were in concentration camp?
Everybody asking me.
And one man, particularly one man, a tall fellow, says to me [GERMAN].
In German, he said it to me.
I shouldn't get so excited.
No.
He got it from me.
I said, what?
I said to him.
I don't know what you are, whether you are Jewish or not Jewish, whether you're German or English.
But I said it and maybe not as good as now.
I don't care.
But you've got nothing to say, because you were here during the war.
You don't know what it was like.
After that, everybody was trying to get hold of me.
They were saying, where was I?
And this and that.
I pushed my way through.
I don't know how.
And I got through, and I went away.
And I was looking for Ellen, for the girl who brought me here.
I said, what did I need that for?
To go to Hyde Park, I should not have all this excitement.
By this time, I was terribly excited, and tears were coming down my face and things.
And I was hiding from everybody.
I go down and look around the whole crowd, and everywhere people were scattering and going and this.
I can't see Ellen.
She brought me, but she left me there, you know, all alone.
I was real-- I couldn't believe that she could do such a thing to me because I wasn't keen to go, you know?
Anyway the end is, I'm standing and waiting and looking in all the directions, and who comes behind me but this old fellow.
And he starts saying to me something like, miss, would you like to come and have a cup of tea?
So I said, I don't drink tea.
I don't speak to strangers.
And I recognized who he was.
I was very cross with him, you know, because I already told him off.
And that was it.
I was walking away, and he went away.
I thought I would never see him anymore.
And then it came around that after-- and I was waiting for this Ellen.
I don't want to go alone Sunday to the hostel to be on my own.
So I thought, she must come back for me.
She can't leave me here.
And, again, I went around.
First, I sat down on the bench-- on a bench.
Then I got up, then started looking.
Couldn't see her.
So, in the end, he came back again, whether I would like to drink some coffee.
I said, I don't drink coffee, and I didn't want to listen anymore to him.
And then I got fed up.
I said, to hell.
I'm going home.
I was getting hungry, too.
So I went to the where the bus stop is.
And I'm going home.
And I'm going on the bus.
Who comes up the bus and sits next to me?
This fellow.
[LAUGHS] And when we-- at first, I didn't talk to him.
I didn't even-- I was looking through the window.
But after a while, I could see that was him.
I said, what is he doing here?
But, anyway, he said to me, you live in a hostel.
And he knew the area is near Cricklewood.
I said, how do you know?
He said, I know some people in the hostel.
He would very much like to meet someone in the hostel who he used to know.
So it's got nothing to do with me.
So I went in the hostel.
He came after me.
Now I'm calling out if there is somebody home, because I don't want to be left with him here alone completely anyway.
So I'm calling out, is anybody home?
Yes.
There was one girl there.
Thank God.
I was so pleased.
So I said, listen, this is here a young man who knows someone here from before.
I was only there, I don't know, two weeks or three weeks and a short while or even shorter.
I said, I'm going upstairs.
I'm very tired.
Can you just sit down and take him to the sitting room and talk to him because I don't know who he means.
And I went upstairs.
But in the meantime, whilst I went upstairs, I quickly went and put on the kettle to make myself cup of tea.
And I went upstairs, and I changed my shoes, put my slippers on, made myself comfortable.
And she was sitting and talking to him.
After a while-- and I was wearing no lipstick.
And in those days, after the war-- and I didn't need it anyway.
I was a young girl.
Anyway.
Suddenly, she calls me down.
She says, Minia, come down a minute.
I said, yes?
What is it?
Minia, he didn't come to talk to anybody.
He only wanted to speak to me.
[LAUGHS] This is what it was.
So I sat down, look, I want to tell you something.
Yeah.
So I said-- she said, did you put the kettle on?
I said, yes.
You sit down.
I make the tea.
She will make the tea now.
So, all right.
We were sitting down.
And I said, but I have to go out because I was made out arrangements with the other girls from which I came over with that day to meet up.
We had a club in Primrose, you know, Primrose Club, which was in Hampstead.
And in the evening, we're all meeting up together.
So what do I need-- so I, I, I said to him, we have got a meeting this evening, and we are going to Hampstead.
So he says, but I live in Hampstead.
He was living in Haverstock Hill, right near Primrose, walking distance.
Oh, may I come as well to this house?
I want to see what it-- what-- how they-- because he heard now that we are all from-- he wanted to come and look at this hostel.
And this is how I got to know Kurt.
He was from Vienna.
And when I arrived, the girls were waiting there.
Rella, who I knew like a sister, she says to me [POLISH] in Polish.
She said, [LAUGHS] like who did I bring here, you know, with me, sort of.
Yeah.
The Primrose Club was really a fantastic thing.
It was specially for us.
And it was taking over two houses to make it into a club.
And we had games.
We, we met up there to play games or to talk.
Or sometimes those had table tennis.
And mainly it was a place where we should meet, we should meet and talk and meet up together.
Oh, quite often.
But mainly-- quite often-- when I say quite often, mainly in the weekend because during the week, we were all working or learning, whatever-- studying, whatever.
And in, in the weekend, Shabbas, Saturday night and Sunday night was the time when we actually really met.
But there was also a place.
If somebody came from another town, like from Manchester, they had a place upstairs where they could put you up to sleep as well for someone-- so that you have a place where to go to.
Until-- I don't know how long it was, actually.
Until it was in existence.
I can't remember how long it was in existence.
Well, actually, with me, it was different.
I got married afterwards.
And then I had children.
To Kurt.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
It was really amazing, because when I met him-- and we were in a hostel, a very religious hostel.

We were not allowed to go out during the week because we had to-- after work, we would come home, have a meal, and then we had to see to our own things like washing and ironing, whatever we needed to do for ourselves.
And in the evening, we were not allowed to go out anyway.
The only time we could go out was Saturday night and Sunday night.
And we had a very rigid matron.
Oh.
Miss Gostle, she was as big as this room nearly.
I mean, big woman.
And a very, very-- she was very bossy.
And we knew the rules.
She told us the rules, what we can do, what we can't do.
So we knew that we can't go out in the evening.
Well, when I met up with Kurt on Sunday, he telephoned Sunday evening.
Wait a moment.
Sunday evening.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Yeah.
It's was Sunday.
No, not Sunday.
Monday.
He telephone Monday evening whether I could come out for a little while just to talk to him.
So I said, I cannot come out.
I'm not allowed.
I know my rules.
And I'm not going to start saying anything to my matron because she wouldn't allow me.
He carried on so long and so long that I said, well, look.
I'm not going to ask her.
If you want to speak to her, you speak to her.
But he had so much charm.
He was such a charming man.
When he started to speak to her-- and I knew that she would never let me go out.
As soon as he spoke to her, she says, yes, Minia, you can go.
She let me go out.
So we went up and down for a walk.
And he was telling me, you know, that he is going away now on a journey with his boss, because he  was only a young man in those days.
And he would be traveling.
They'll be going all over the places-- I don't know-- as far as Manchester or further.
And in the meantime, I shouldn't go out with anybody.
[LAUGHS] This is how he--
And, apparently, years later, when I-- when I got-- when I got married, my father-in-law-- he was such a nice, distinguished-- I'll show you a picture of him.
Really nice man, he was.
He-- also from Vienna.
In Vienna, he was on the stock exchange.
And in-- and in here, he was working for El Al.
He was a chief executive in El Al.
First, he was with [?
Sim ?] Airlines.
But when they split-- [?
Sim ?] Airlines and El Al-- they wanted them both.
They wanted him at [?
Sim, ?] and they wanted him at El Al.
But it so happens he couldn't be at both, so he chose El Al.
But 25 years later, they made him such a-- they brought him to Israel to a top hotel with his wife.
And it was-- they made a big fuss of him.
He was a great man, really, a hard worker, very nice man.
Well, anyway, he told me when we got married.
He says, you know, Miniale-- he always called me Miniale.
Miniale, should I tell you something?
The first time Kurt came to see me, he said he's met the girl he's going to marry, you know?
Just goes to show you he was very much after me.
In fact, there was a time when I wanted to go and see my sister.
And that was very difficult, not only financially, but also you couldn't go to Germany in those days.
It was-- the letters were going through BO-- BAOR, through the army.
And it was very difficult.
And I didn't have a passport.
I was an alien.
I didn't have-- I wasn't-- you know, I didn't have citizenship in England.
I was allowed to stay in England.
Well, anyway, he arranged everything for me.
He did everything because he could speak English perfectly.
I mean, he was an educated man, anyway.
And he arranged everything.
In the end, before I went to Israel-- to my sister, to Germany, he wanted to get engaged.
And I said to him, Kurt, I will not get engaged until I see my sister.
Because we were that sort of family, you know?
My sister is older than me, and I had to-- you know, and I didn't know.
So I want-- I loved him very much.
But I, I didn't-- I come from a big family.
The only sister I've got, and I, I wanted to go to Israel.
And they wanted me to come with them to Israel.
It was such a difficult thing in those days, you see?
At the very first, I couldn't be with my sister because I was very ill.
And then when now, maybe I could have had the chance.
So when I said to him, let's wait till I come back from Germany.
He was afraid I might not come back.
So he was getting excited.
He said, well, if you that's how you feel about it.
I said, well, I would rather have it that way.
And sure enough, he got cross.
But he came back.
And he said, all right.
We'll wait.
And, and I went to my sister.
And, of course, she wrote such fantastic letters, my sister.
Didn't know what to do, whether she should-- it was a difficult decision for her because he was very much-- he had all his papers ready.
He wanted to come to Germany.
But by that time, I was already going on back to England.
I had already everything arranged to come back.
In any case--
My sister said, she can't really.
She doesn't know what to do.
Minia, she said to me, I don't know.
I mean, I would like you so much to be with me, but what am I to do?
I don't know.
It must be my decision, you know?
And my brother-in-law wanted me so much.
I should there very much.
Well anyway, in the end, she gave me everything what I need for marriage-- sheets and pillowcases and tablecloths.
And you'll never believe what she gave me.
She gave me a sewing machine.
She got hold of a sewing machine there, because she knew I will need it.
Maybe I will need to earn the money for corsetry.
And so much stuff.
And China.
And, oh, everything.
Because my brother-in-law was quite a capable man.
And after the war, he started doing a bit of business with the Germans.
Everything she wanted to give me, whatever.
By the time I came back, there was so much stuff I couldn't manage to get it all on the train in time.
And I wasn't allowed to miss the, the train, because if I do, I'm an alien.
They wouldn't let me-- I couldn't anymore come into England.
So everything remained in, in, in Ostend.
The whole stuff.
I arrived with nothing.
When I arrived-- and when I let him know that I was arriving to Victoria, he came running like mad to Victoria.
I had swollen eyes, crying that all my stuff is in Ostend.
But he arranged it all and had it all- everything was given back.
That means we had to go to, to-- from Ostend, it arrived to Dover.
I mean, I arrived in Victoria, but the stuff arrived in Dover.
And I had to go one day to Dover to claim all my things.
And he came with me, because he could speak English.
He told them I'm from the concentration camp.
My sister gave me all of this for the we-- they let me through with a lot [LAUGHS] because I was afraid to take a sewing machine.
Anyway, this was that.
And then when I got back, straightaway we got engaged.
His mother made me really a nice engagement.
And then we got married the 7th of November.
I was, in 1948 in Germany, Pesach time.
I don't know.
It was March, April.
And I got married the 7th of November.
Yes, it was a really nice wedding.
I mean, we didn't have music or, or you know?
But my mother-in-law made it really nice.
Most of the things were all baked by hand.
Because she was from Vienna, she could bake so beautiful.
Everybody was saying how wonderful everything was, you know?
This was more of a reception, not dinner, you know?
But beautifully prepared and done.
Everything was very, very nice.
And we went away.
We could only afford to go away for two nights.
To Brighton, we went.
You know where we went?
To that hotel where it was bombed.
[LAUGHS] What was it called?
In Brighton.
I've forgotten.
Grand Hotel.
Went to the Grand Hotel.
But it was nice.
Everything was so wonderful, and we had children, and everything was fine.
And then we went-- after 17 years, we went to live in Italy.
That was the highlight of my life.
In Italy, we had a good life, very nice life.
But my husband got very ill.
And it didn't work out well.
He died.
And then-- that was, we came back to England first.
Business was also going down.
He was ill.
It was very difficult.
And then afterwards, I had my downfall again.
I was somewhere up there, and then I went right down again.
This is when I started learning it was useful for me to learn corsetry-- that I knew corsetry, because I started working in corsetry.
That means I was mainly selling and doing.
But it was good that it I knew it.
